event: The Virgin and Child enthroned in glory
symbols: dark blue maphorion — Mary's heavenly queenship and the depth of the divine mystery she bears
Christ Child raising His hand — the gesture of blessing and divine authority even in infancy
golden halos — divine glory surrounding both Mother and Son
angels with colored wings — the heavenly court attending the Theotokos
Slavonic monograms — the traditional abbreviation for Mother of God (MP OY)
golden background — the uncreated light of God in Orthodox theology
description: An Orthodox-style icon depicting the Virgin Mary standing, wearing a deep blue maphorion with gold trim over a dark garment. She holds the Christ Child on her left arm. The child wears white swaddling and raises his right hand in a gesture of blessing while his left hand extends outward. Both figures are surrounded by luminous golden halos. Two small angels with colored wings appear in the upper corners. Slavonic monograms are inscribed at the top. The background is brilliant gold, and the overall composition follows the Hodegetria type.
theological_description: This icon follows the Hodegetria ('She who shows the Way') type, in which the Theotokos presents the Christ Child to the world. Mary's role is not to draw attention to herself but to point toward her Son — she is the way-shower, directing all humanity to Christ. The Child's blessing gesture affirms His divine authority: even as an infant, He is the Logos through whom all things were made (JHN.1.3). The Slavonic monograms MP OY (Meter Theou, Mother of God) affirm the Christological definition of the Council of Ephesus (431), which declared Mary Theotokos not to elevate her but to safeguard the truth of the Incarnation — that the one she bore is truly God. The gold background represents the uncreated light of Tabor, the divine glory that permeates all reality. The angels attending the scene remind the viewer that the Incarnation is a cosmic event: 'the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory' (JHN.1.14).

scripture_refs.reason: The Holy Spirit overshadowing Mary, resulting in the divine Child she holds
The Word became flesh — the Incarnation made visible in the icon of Mother and divine Child
All things were made through Him — the infant's blessing hand reveals His cosmic authority
Elizabeth's exclamation: the mother of my Lord — affirming the divine maternity
